name: MirrorMind
shortDesc: A journaling AI that reflects your thinking back — structured Socratic questioning, not answers.
longDesc: Most AI tools give you answers. MirrorMind does the opposite — it reads what you wrote and responds with a question designed to push your own thinking one layer deeper. Built on the idea that clarity comes from within, not from a chatbot. The system uses a question-tree structure where each response is chosen based on the emotional and logical pattern of your entry. The goal is to be the tool Al-Ghazali would have used for muhasaba — self-accounting.

name: IslamicOS
shortDesc: A productivity system built entirely around Islamic time — Fajr to Isha, task blocks, spiritual checkpoints.
longDesc: Modern productivity tools are built on a secular 9-to-5 assumption. IslamicOS rebuilds the stack from scratch: the day starts at Fajr, focus blocks are shaped around prayer times, and there are reflection checkpoints built into the flow. It is not just a scheduler — it embeds the Islamic concept of barakah (blessing in time) into how tasks are prioritised and reviewed. Think Notion meets a prayer timetable API meets a personal Wird tracker.

name: GhazaliNet
shortDesc: An AI reader for classical Islamic texts — surfaces cross-references between Al-Ghazali, Ibn Arabi, and Rumi.
longDesc: The great Islamic scholars wrote in conversation with each other across centuries, but those threads are invisible unless you already know where to look. GhazaliNet is a semantic search and cross-reference engine trained on classical texts. You highlight a passage in Ihya Ulum al-Din and it surfaces the parallel concept in the Masnavi or the Fusus al-Hikam. Designed for students of Islamic philosophy who want to do serious textual research without a decade of prerequisite reading.

name: GeoPulse
shortDesc: A live geopolitical tracker that scores sources on credibility and flags narrative conflicts across outlets.
longDesc: Mainstream news gives you a single narrative framed as fact. GeoPulse treats every news event as a contested object and maps the narrative distance between sources — how differently is Al Jazeera, Reuters, and a primary government document describing the same event? It uses the source-ladder methodology: primary documents rank highest, then forensic/OSINT, then investigative journalism, then wire services. The output is not a summary — it is a conflict map that shows you where the disagreements are and what each side is claiming.
